source_metadata.title_raw: Peter Lambert - A-18-168 - October 3, 2018 - Muir Beach

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 3, 2018

Peter Lambert
21 Cove Lane
Muir Beach, CA 94965-9717

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-18-168

Dear Mr. Lambert:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090 we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Marin County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Are you prohibited from making a monetary donation in your private capacity to the Muir Beach Community Services District to help cover the costs of resurfacing an existing street on which you own a residence and two other non-income producing homes?

2. Are you prohibited from participating in decisions related to resurfacing that street and replacing the water line beneath it?

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-168
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Based upon the facts provided, your contemplated donation is neither subject to the
Act's conflict of interest provisions nor Section 1090.

2. No. Notwithstanding the fact that you live on a street that would be resurfaced and have the
water line beneath it replaced, you are not prohibited from taking part in the decisions
related to these repairs by the Act or Section 1090.

FACTS

You are an elected member of the Board of Directors of the Muir Beach Community
Services District ("MBCSD"), which has, in varying degrees, assumed responsibility for
maintaining Muir Beach's roads that are not maintained by Marin County or the State. Muir Beach
is located in an unincorporated area of the County and is governed by MBCSD.

Several years ago, you built a new house in Muir Beach on Sunset Way. Sunset Way is the
most populated street in lower Muir Beach. At that time, you mentioned to the MBCSD District
Manager, who is now an elected Boardmember, that you would like to help with the cost of repairs
to Sunset Way because you recognized that dump and cement trucks would contribute to wear on
the road. The MBCSD has long recognized that eventually Sunset Way will need to be repaired and
repaved and that it would be desirable to concurrently replace the water supply main lines, which
run along under Sunset Way.

Muir Beach has approximately 300 residents. In addition to your residence, you co-own two
additional houses on Sunset Way. None of the properties you own are income producing properties.
You believe these houses will also benefit from the repairs to Sunset Way. You would like to cover
some part of the cost of resurfacing Sunset Way between your residence and Highway 1. Three of
the other four MBCSD Boardmembers also own and reside in houses on Sunset Way. The
remaining Boardmember lives in and owns a house on another MBCSD maintained street.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. (Section 87103.) Different
standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be
material depending on the nature of the interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise
are defined in Section 87103.

According to the facts presented, you anticipate making a monetary donation to the MBCSD
for the purpose of covering a portion of costs related to resurfacing Sunset Way. This donation does
not involve a governmental decision and is not subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions.
(See Tobias Advice Letter, No. A-17-272.) However, we further analyze whether you may
participate in the decisions before the MBCSD concerning the road repair of Sunset Way and
replacement of the water supply main lines that run beneath it.

File No. A-18-168
Page No. 3

Under Sections 82033 and 87103(b), an official has an interest in any real property owned
directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate family, if the
interest has a fair market value of $2,000 or more. Based upon the facts provided, your only
interests at issue with respect to the decisions relating to the resurfacing of Sunset Way and the
replacement of the water lines beneath it are your real property interests in your residence and two
other homes located on Sunset Way.² Accordingly, we must determine whether there is a
foreseeable and material financial effect on these real property interests from decisions related to
the repairs to Sunset Way.

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest.
(Regulation 18702.) The materiality standards for any particular interest are provided in
Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5.

However, Regulation 18702.2(c)(1) provides that a financial effect of a decision
is not material if the decision "solely concerns repairs, replacement or maintenance of
existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities." (See also
Torres Advice Letter, A-17-061.) Decisions to resurface Sunset Way and replace the
water supply main lines which run under Sunset Way would be solely to repair, replace
and maintain existing infrastructure. Therefore, the effect of such decisions on your
property interests is not material and you would not have a conflict under the Act when
participating in the decisions.

Section 1090

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates
Section 1090 is void, regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all
parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is applicable to one
member of a governing body of a public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the
interested board member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the
contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.)

The resurfacing of Sunset Way and replacement of water supply main lines will most likely
involve a contract between MBCSD and a contractor to perform the work. Although Section 1090
does not specifically define "financial interest," the term has been liberally interpreted. (See People
v. Deysher (1934) 2 Cal.2d 141, 146.) "The government's right to the absolute, undivided
allegiance of a public officer is diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of
personal financial gain as where he acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th
1271, 1298 (citations omitted).)

² For purposes of this letter, we assume you have an interest of at least $2,000 or more in each property.

File No. A-18-168
Page No. 4

Nonetheless, an official has a financial interest in a contract only when there is a sufficient connection between the contract in question and the interest held by the official. Under this formulation of "financial interest," it would be a clear Section 1090 conflict of interest if you had a financial relationship with, or interest in, a potential contractor to provide services, or a financial relationship with a contracting entity. (Collins Advice Letter No. A-17-184.) You would not have a financial interest in a MBCSD contract as contemplated by Section 1090 simply because your properties, in addition to other properties along Sunset Way (the most populated street in lower Muir Beach), will peripherally benefit from the resurfacing of Sunset Way or the replacement of the water lines beneath it. Based on the facts provided, we conclude that Section 1090 does not apply.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl
